quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Operon"?

options.text: Speciation driven by geographic isolation that prevents gene flow between populations, allowing independent evolution
Differential survival and reproduction of individuals with favorable heritable traits in a given environment; the primary mechanism of adaptive evolution
A cluster of prokaryotic genes under control of a single promoter and operator, transcribed as one polycistronic mRNA (e.g., lac operon, trp operon)
The pathway by which a chemical element (C, N, P, H₂O) moves through the biotic and abiotic components of an ecosystem

quiz_questions.explanation: "Operon" is defined as: A cluster of prokaryotic genes under control of a single promoter and operator, transcribed as one polycistronic mRNA (e.g., lac operon, trp operon). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Transcription factor"?

options.text: A protein that binds to regulatory DNA sequences (enhancers, silencers, promoters) to activate or repress transcription of specific genes
A series of protein kinases that sequentially phosphorylate and activate each other, amplifying the signal at each step
Insertion or deletion of nucleotides (not in multiples of 3) that shifts the reading frame, altering all downstream amino acids
The main replication enzyme in E. coli; synthesizes DNA 5'→3' by adding nucleotides complementary to the template strand

quiz_questions.explanation: "Transcription factor" is defined as: A protein that binds to regulatory DNA sequences (enhancers, silencers, promoters) to activate or repress transcription of specific genes. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Epigenetics"?

options.text: Heritable changes in gene expression without altering the DNA sequence; includes DNA methylation (usually silencing) and histone modification
A molecule resembling the substrate that competes for the active site; increases apparent Km but does not affect Vmax (can be overcome by more substrate)
The percentage of offspring showing recombinant phenotypes; used to calculate the distance between linked genes
A three-nucleotide sequence in mRNA that codes for a specific amino acid or stop signal; 64 total codons, 61 code for amino acids

quiz_questions.explanation: "Epigenetics" is defined as: Heritable changes in gene expression without altering the DNA sequence; includes DNA methylation (usually silencing) and histone modification. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Chromatin remodeling"?

options.text: A series of protein kinases that sequentially phosphorylate and activate each other, amplifying the signal at each step
The energy available to do work; ΔG  0 (endergonic, requires energy input), ΔG = 0 (equilibrium)
The end product of a metabolic pathway inhibits an early enzyme in the pathway, preventing overproduction (e.g., isoleucine inhibiting threonine deaminase)
Changes in chromatin structure that affect gene accessibility: histone acetylation opens chromatin (activating), deacetylation compacts it (silencing)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Chromatin remodeling" is defined as: Changes in chromatin structure that affect gene accessibility: histone acetylation opens chromatin (activating), deacetylation compacts it (silencing). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "microRNA (miRNA)"?

options.text: A measure of disorder or randomness; the 2nd law of thermodynamics states that entropy of the universe increases in spontaneous processes
A 3-carbon sugar produced by the Calvin cycle; the direct product used to build glucose, amino acids, and fatty acids
Small non-coding RNA molecules (~22 nucleotides) that bind to complementary mRNA sequences, targeting them for degradation or blocking translation
Using the exergonic hydrolysis of ATP to drive an endergonic reaction, often through phosphorylation of a substrate

quiz_questions.explanation: "microRNA (miRNA)" is defined as: Small non-coding RNA molecules (~22 nucleotides) that bind to complementary mRNA sequences, targeting them for degradation or blocking translation. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A cluster of prokaryotic genes under control of a single promoter and operator, transcribed as one polycistronic mRNA (e.g., lac operon, trp operon)"?

options.text: Electron transport chain
Operon
Genotype frequency
Exponential growth

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Operon".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A protein that binds to regulatory DNA sequences (enhancers, silencers, promoters) to activate or repress transcription of specific genes"?

options.text: Recombinant DNA
Transcription factor
Photorespiration
Phospholipid

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Transcription factor".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Heritable changes in gene expression without altering the DNA sequence; includes DNA methylation (usually silencing) and histone modification"?

options.text: Epigenetics
FADH₂
Law of Segregation
Endomembrane system

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Epigenetics".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Changes in chromatin structure that affect gene accessibility: histone acetylation opens chromatin (activating), deacetylation compacts it (silencing)"?

options.text: Chromatin remodeling
CRISPR-Cas9
Angiogenesis
Oxidative phosphorylation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Chromatin remodeling".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Small non-coding RNA molecules (~22 nucleotides) that bind to complementary mRNA sequences, targeting them for degradation or blocking translation"?

options.text: Chitin
Species richness
microRNA (miRNA)
Net primary productivity (NPP)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "microRNA (miRNA)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Gene Regulation is accurate?

options.text: Mutations can be spontaneous (replication errors) or induced by mutagens.
Water is split (photolysis) at PSII, releasing O₂.
Gene Regulation controls when, where, and how much of a gene product is made.
In eukaryotes, pre-mRNA undergoes processing: 5' cap addition, 3' poly-A tail, and splicing to remove introns and join exons.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Gene Regulation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Gene Regulation?

options.text: In prokaryotes, the lac operon model demonstrates inducible gene regulation: the repressor blocks transcription until lactose (inducer) binds and removes it, while CAP protein enhances transcription when glucose is low.
The Knudson two-hit hypothesis explains that both copies of a tumor suppressor must be inactivated for cancer to develop.
The central dogma of molecular biology—DNA → RNA → Protein—describes the flow of genetic information in cells.
DNA Replication and Repair describes the semiconservative process by which DNA is copied before cell division.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Gene Regulation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Gene Regulation?

options.text: In eukaryotes, regulation occurs at multiple levels: chromatin remodeling (acetylation, methylation), transcription factors, mRNA processing, mRNA stability, translation regulation, and post-translational modification.
When damage is too severe, p53 activates apoptosis to prevent propagation of mutations that could lead to cancer.
Monosaccharides join via glycosidic bonds through dehydration synthesis to form disaccharides and polysaccharides.
Helicase unwinds the double helix, primase synthesizes RNA primers, and DNA polymerase III synthesizes new strands in the 5'→3' direction.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Gene Regulation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Gene Regulation?

options.text: Adaptive radiation (rapid diversification from a single ancestor into multiple ecological niches) often follows mass extinctions or colonization of new environments.
Saturated vs. unsaturated fatty acids differ in the presence of C=C double bonds, affecting fluidity and melting point.
Gene Regulation controls when, where, and how much of a gene product is made.
Helicase unwinds the double helix, primase synthesizes RNA primers, and DNA polymerase III synthesizes new strands in the 5'→3' direction.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Gene Regulation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Gene Regulation?

options.text: Properties of Water make it essential for life due to its polar covalent bonds and hydrogen bonding.
Nucleic Acids (DNA and RNA) store and transmit genetic information through sequences of nucleotide monomers.
Populations are characterized by density, age structure, survivorship curves (Type I, II, III), and life history strategies (r-selected vs.

In prokaryotes, the lac operon model demonstrates inducible gene regulation: the repressor blocks transcription until lactose (inducer) binds and removes it, while CAP protein enhances transcription when glucose is low.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Gene Regulation as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Gene Regulation?

options.text: Cancer epigenetics: abnormal DNA methylation patterns silencing tumor suppressor genes or activating oncogenes without DNA mutations
Feedback inhibition in the cholesterol synthesis pathway: statins mimic the substrate of HMG-CoA reductase, acting as competitive inhibitors to lower cholesterol levels
Drug design targeting specific enzyme active sites: HIV protease inhibitors blocking viral protein processing to treat AIDS
Genetic rescue of the Florida panther: introducing 8 female Texas pumas in 1995 restored genetic diversity and tripled the population from ~25 to ~200 individuals

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Gene Regulation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Gene Regulation?

options.text: Stem cell differentiation controlled by sequential gene regulation, with the same genome producing >200 different specialized cell types in the human body
Endosymbiotic theory evidence: mitochondria and chloroplasts have double membranes, own circular DNA, 70S ribosomes, and replicate independently
Capillary action in plant xylem vessels transporting water against gravity from roots to leaves through cohesion-adhesion-tension
Down syndrome (trisomy 21) caused by nondisjunction during meiosis, resulting in an extra copy of chromosome 21 in all cells

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Gene Regulation.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Gene Regulation applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Ecosystem services valuation estimating that pollination by insects is worth $235-577 billion annually globally, providing economic justification for pollinator conservation
Lac operon as a model for understanding antibiotic resistance gene regulation in bacteria, where environmental signals trigger expression of resistance genes
Industrial enzyme applications: proteases in detergents, amylases in food processing, and DNA polymerase in PCR for forensics and diagnostics
Forensic genetics using Mendelian inheritance patterns to establish paternity or identify family relationships from DNA evidence

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Gene Regulation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Gene Regulation?

options.text: CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing using guide RNA complementary base pairing to target specific DNA sequences for precise genetic modification
Cancer epigenetics: abnormal DNA methylation patterns silencing tumor suppressor genes or activating oncogenes without DNA mutations
Genetic engineering using the universal genetic code to express human insulin in E. coli bacteria, producing recombinant insulin for diabetes treatment
CRISPR-Cas9 clinical trials treating sickle cell disease by editing the BCL11A gene in patient stem cells to reactivate fetal hemoglobin production

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Gene Regulation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Gene Regulation in practice?

options.text: Stem cell differentiation controlled by sequential gene regulation, with the same genome producing >200 different specialized cell types in the human body
α-amanitin (death cap mushroom toxin) inhibiting RNA polymerase II, blocking transcription and causing fatal liver failure within days
Forensic genetics using Mendelian inheritance patterns to establish paternity or identify family relationships from DNA evidence
Biomagnification of mercury and DDT through food chains: top predators (eagles, tuna) accumulate the highest concentrations, explaining why large predatory fish have mercury advisories

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Gene Regulation.
